original_abstract: When Nana Osei-Opare asked to reprint my article, “The Third International and the Struggle for National Liberation in South Africa,” I balked. I was only twenty-three when I wrote it for a graduate seminar at UCLA, and like most preliminary research projects it was based on a slim body of primary sources since I had no access to South African archives. I had just settled on an ambitious and unrealistic dissertation project comparing the histories of the Black Left in South Africa and the U.S. South—unrealistic because I had no prospect of getting into South Africa. In 1985, President P. W. Botha declared a state of emergency, and my participation in protests at the South African Consulate in Beverly Hills did not bode well for my visa application. Looking back at the essay three decades later, I find some of it downright embarrassing—particularly the occasional flights into rigid Marxist language, and the limited sources available to me. Of course, in 1986, the Soviet Union was three years from collapse and the Soviet Archives were several years from opening. But considering these constraints, and the audacity of youth, there are things about the article of which I’m proud. Taking a page from Cedric Robinson, whose book Black Marxism had just appeared in 1983, I suggest that a Black radical tradition rooted in earlier notions of African redemption, rural opposition culminating in resistance to the Land Act of 1913, and expressions of working-class and petit bourgeois African nationalism had already produced a vision of self-determination. These movements preceded the formation of the Communist Party of South Africa (CPSA), whose initial orientation was toward the white working class. Imagine a Communist Party anywhere back ing striking miners under the banner, “Workers of the World Unite and Fight for a WHITE SOUTH AFRICA!” When African trade unionists, as well as officials of the Communist International (Comintern) in Moscow, criticized Party leaders for their uncritical support of the Rand Revolt in 1922, to the exclusion of the more numerous African miners’ struggles, white South African
humanized_abstract: Last week I was approached by Nana Osei-Opare who requested permission to reprint an article that I wrote for a graduate seminar at UCLA some 30 years ago, “The Third International and the Struggle for National Liberation in South Africa,” the first attempt at exploring the international dynamics that made black rebellion possible in South Africa. I was then only 23 years old and it was an exercise based on an inadequate number of primary sources because at the time, I did not have access to the archives in South Africa. At the time, I had also contemplated a dissertation that I considered revolutionary, or at least ambitious, which was to compare and contrast the history of the Black Left in the U.S. South and in South Africa. Although President P.W. Botha declared a State of Emergency in 1985, and I had already had some run-in with the law for “violent” activity against the South African Consulate in Beverly Hills, I didn’t really have any doubts about my ineligibility for a visa to South Africa at the time, so my proposed dissertation topic seemed quite quixotic. Rereading my 23-year-old efforts a month ago, for the first time in decades, I must say that while it’s clear how embarrassing and over-explains in communist-speak some of the early sections are – given the sources available at the time – it is in other respects not at all embarrassing. There’s even a sense in which I’m particularly proud of certain elements of the work, given the time that it was written. Perhaps this is just the effect of being farther away from youth and having more regard for the original work than for one’s early attempts at critical reflection. At any rate, it is an interesting early work because it takes certain theses – for example, that an African Black radical tradition is possible only on the basis of different understandings of Africa’s redemption than those which have heretofore characterized black people’s conceptions of their world. That is, the rural resistance to land expropriation in Africa that began at least as far back as the resistance to the South African government’s Land Act of 1913 — the legislation which “awarded” African reserves to black South Africans — plus the developing black working-class and petit bourgeois nationalisms which are a product of that culture — all give evidence of a tradition which looks toward some kind of African self-determination. These phenomena occur before the founding of the Communist Party of South Africa, which, as a white-led party oriented to the needs of white labor, might with equal historical probability have marched striking miners behind the slogan of “Workers of the World Unite — and Fight for a WHITE SOUTH AFRICA!” Indeed, the African unionists who protested to the Comintern officials in Moscow over the CPSA leadership’s misjudgment of the significance of the strike by black mineworkers on the Rand in 1922 as being inadequate to the “real issue,” were vindicated in their criticism.
